task.task_description: This is a comparative detector technology evaluation task focused on precision timing performance for calorimetry applications. The user wants to design and simulate three different calorimeter detector concepts to determine which provides the best timing resolution in the 10-30 picosecond range while maintaining adequate energy resolution. This involves running Geant4 simulations of each detector type (LYSO crystal, plastic scintillator tiles, and tungsten/plastic sampling calorimeter) with electron beams at different energies, then analyzing the timing characteristics of detector hits to extract timing resolution from first-hit time distributions. The task combines detector physics simulation with performance benchmarking for future collider applications where fast timing is critical for pileup rejection.
task.goal: Determine which of three detector concepts (LYSO crystal, plastic scintillator, or sampling calorimeter) provides the best timing performance with 10-30 picosecond resolution while maintaining reasonable energy resolution for pileup rejection at future colliders

constraints.description: Must test exactly 3 configurations (one per concept)
Timing resolution target of approximately 10-30 picoseconds
Energy sweep limited to 1, 5, 20 GeV for tractable simulation
Must use electrons (e-) in Geant4 particle gun
LYSO must use homogeneous_slab or box topology
Plastic scintillator must use segmented topology
Sampling calorimeter must use box or shashlik topology with tungsten absorber

task.context:
Design a calorimeter capable of precision timing measurements with resolution
of approximately 10-30 picoseconds. Compare different detector concepts for timing performance.

**CONFIGURATIONS TO TEST:**
1. Fast crystal: LYSO (Lu2SiO5:Ce) homogeneous calorimeter — fast scintillation (~40ns decay),
   high light yield (~30,000 photons/MeV). Use "homogeneous_slab" or "box" topology.
2. Plastic scintillator tiles: Fast plastic scintillator (BC-408 style, ~2ns decay time).
   Use "segmented" topology with scintillator tiles.
3. Sampling calorimeter: Tungsten absorber + thin plastic scintillator layers.
   Use "box" or "shashlik" topology with tungsten absorber.

**PRACTICAL CONSTRAINTS (for tractable simulation):**
- Energy sweep: use particle_energy: [1, 5, 20] (GeV)
- Use e- (electrons) in Geant4 particle gun

**MEASURE for each concept:**
1. Hit time distribution — compute timing resolution from spread of first-hit times
   (use the 'time' column in hit data, which is in nanoseconds)
2. Energy resolution at each energy point
3. Dependence of timing precision on particle energy
4. Number of hits and signal amplitude

**GOAL:**
Determine which detector concept provides the best timing performance while maintaining
reasonable calorimetric energy resolution. Fast timing is critical for pileup rejection
at future colliders.

Test exactly 3 configurations (one per concept).

steps.name: generate_lyso_geometry
steps.description: Generate GDML geometry for LYSO crystal homogeneous calorimeter with 20x20x20 cm³ volume for electromagnetic shower containment

steps.name: generate_plastic_geometry
steps.description: Generate GDML geometry for segmented plastic scintillator calorimeter with 5x5 cm² tiles

steps.name: generate_sampling_geometry
steps.description: Generate GDML geometry for tungsten/plastic sampling calorimeter with alternating layers

steps.name: simulate_lyso_sweep
steps.description: Run Geant4 simulation of LYSO calorimeter with electron beams at 1, 5, 20 GeV to measure timing and energy response

steps.name: simulate_plastic_sweep
steps.description: Run Geant4 simulation of plastic scintillator calorimeter with electron beams at 1, 5, 20 GeV

steps.name: simulate_sampling_sweep
steps.description: Run Geant4 simulation of tungsten/plastic sampling calorimeter with electron beams at 1, 5, 20 GeV

steps.name: analyze_lyso_timing
steps.description: Extract timing resolution from LYSO first-hit time distributions and energy resolution at each energy point

steps.name: analyze_plastic_timing
steps.description: Extract timing resolution from plastic scintillator first-hit time distributions and energy resolution

steps.name: analyze_sampling_timing
steps.description: Extract timing resolution from sampling calorimeter first-hit time distributions and energy resolution

steps.name: compare_detector_performance
steps.description: Compare timing and energy resolution across all three detector concepts to identify optimal design for 10-30 ps timing

steps.name: visualize_timing_results
steps.description: Create publication-quality plots showing timing resolution vs energy for all three detectors and example time distributions

steps.name: generate_final_report
steps.description: Synthesize all results into final report recommending optimal detector for 10-30 ps timing at future colliders

plan.reasoning: Workflow systematically tests three detector concepts using energy sweeps for efficient parallel simulation. Analysis focuses on extracting timing resolution from first-hit distributions and comparing to 10-30 ps target while monitoring energy resolution as secondary metric.
